coredata.dc:title: Oxygen vacancy mediated charge transfer expediting over GQDs/TiO2 for enhancing photocatalytic removal of Cr (VI) and RhB synchronously
coredata.dc:creator: Bai, Xue
Ji, Yali
She, Mengyao
Li, Quanquan
Wan, Kerou
Li, Zhuo
Liu, Enzhou
Li, Jianli
coredata.dc:description: Understanding the underlying transport mechanism of photogenerated carriers is crucial in designing active heterojunctions in the field of photocatalysis. In this study, the redox activity of Graphene quantum dots/TiO2 (GQDs/TiO2) hybrid was investigated through water treatment under visible light irradiation. The corresponding findings showed that the charge transfer between GQDs and TiO2 accompanied with oxygen vacancy (V o) was expedited, resulting in enhanced redox activity in the Cr (VI) and RhB co-removing system. Further investigation revealed that V o introduced a defect level in TiO2 just below its conduction band that could induce visible light excitation while leading to quick charge separation and effective transmission from GQDs to TiO2. Therefore, carrying out photocatalytic Cr (VI) reduction and RhB degradation synchronously over GQDs/TiO2 can roughly enhance 2 and 3 times pure TiO2 nanosheets under visible light irradiation. The findings of this study may provide novel insight into heterojunction engineering and defect level controlling in order to attain efficient photocatalyst and offer additional prospects for photocatalytic applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: oxygen vacancy
nano heterojunction
TiO2
GQDs
transmission of carriers

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21676213 , 21807087 and 22077099 ), the Natural Science Basic Research Plan in Shaanxi Province of China (No. 2021JQ-440 and 2020JM-422 ), Key Research and Development Program of Shaanxi (No. 2019KWZ-07 ), and the Key Science and Technology Innovation Team of Shaanxi Province (No. 2017KCT-37 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns and (b) Characteristic Raman spectra of TiO2 nanosheets,

(a) EPR spectra of TiO2 nanosheets and GQDs/ TiO2 hybrid. Inset in (a): enlarged EPR spectrum of pure TiO2. (b) Comparison of FTIR spectra, (c) the magnified view of a portion of the spectrum of TiO2 nanosheets, GQDs and their hybrid samples. (d) Bonding mechanism diagram over GQDs/ TiO2 hybrid. (e) Zeta potential and Core level XPS spectra of different samples: (f) Ti 2p, (g) O1s spectra for GQDs/TiO2 hybrid and TiO2, (h) C1s spectra for GQDs/TiO2 hybrid and GQD.

(a-b) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of TiO2 nanosheets. (c) TEM image and corresponding size distribution inset, (d) XRD pattern and (e) HRTEM of panel c of GQDs, (f) TEM, (g) HRTEM image and (h) AFM image with inset showing the height profile at the range of integration (in the white dotted line box) of GQDs/TiO2 hybrid.

(a) UV–Vis DRS spectra of TiO2 nanosheets, GQDs and GQDs/ TiO2 hybrid with the corresponding physical photograph shown in the inset, (b) Kubelka-Munk plots converted from the UV–vis DRS spectra of TiO2 and GQDs, (c) Comparison of PL spectra excited 405 nm of P25, TiO2 and GQDs/ TiO2, (d-e) XPS valence band spectra and (f) Schematic diagram of band structure for TiO2 and GQDs.
Comparison of photocatalytic activity of P25, TiO2 and GQDs/ TiO2 under visible light irradiation for simultaneous removal of (a) Cr (VI) and (b) RhB.
(a) TRPL spectra excited with 405 nm picosecond pulsed laser and (b) SPV spectra for TiO2 and GQD/ TiO2.
(a) Photocurrent responses of the TiO2 and GQD/ TiO2 under visible light (400–800 nm) irradiation, (b) Nyquist plots for TiO2 and GQD/ TiO2.

(a-c) The Top views and charge density difference of the GQD/ TiO2 heterojunction. Blue, red, light pink, and brown spheres represent Ti, O, H and C atoms, respectively. (d) The possible charge transmission path of GQDs/TiO2 heterojunction under visible and UV light irradiation.
Schematic illustration of the preparation of GQDs/TiO2 composite.
TRPL Lifetimes and the respective proportion of TiO2 and GQD/ TiO2.

head.ce:title: Oxygen vacancy mediated charge transfer expediting over GQDs/TiO2 for enhancing photocatalytic removal of Cr (VI) and RhB synchronously

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Xue Bai and Yali Ji contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Schematic of the main charge transfer mode and subsequent photoreaction process on the GQDs/TiO2 heterojunction under visible light irradiation.
ga1

Understanding the underlying transport mechanism of photogenerated carriers is crucial in designing active heterojunctions in the field of photocatalysis. In this study, the redox activity of Graphene quantum dots/TiO2 (GQDs/TiO2) hybrid was investigated through water treatment under visible light irradiation. The corresponding findings showed that the charge transfer between GQDs and TiO2 accompanied with oxygen vacancy (V o) was expedited, resulting in enhanced redox activity in the Cr (VI) and RhB co-removing system. Further investigation revealed that V o introduced a defect level in TiO2 just below its conduction band that could induce visible light excitation while leading to quick charge separation and effective transmission from GQDs to TiO2. Therefore, carrying out photocatalytic Cr (VI) reduction and RhB degradation synchronously over GQDs/TiO2 can roughly enhance 2 and 3 times pure TiO2 nanosheets under visible light irradiation. The findings of this study may provide novel insight into heterojunction engineering and defect level controlling in order to attain efficient photocatalyst and offer additional prospects for photocatalytic applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Natural photosynthesis deals with the use of using solar energy in driving chemical reactions. Accordingly, photocatalysis have made history by utilizing the energy delivered by light and driving reactions [1,2]. Benefiting from the growing awareness of sustainability, photocatalysis has been regarded as a promising way to solve the energy crisis and achieve environmental remediation. In this regard, improvements in photocatalysts should be explored further in order to boost social development [3–5]. Two dimensional (2D) materials have garnered attention for their unique structures, which have been shown to be good at accelerating charge separation for photocatalytic applications [6–10]. Moreover, their quantum effect seems quite significant; their optical and electrical behaviors are both highly sensitive to size [11–14]. Therefore, the construction of a lower dimension heterojunction may serve as a key factor in enhancing the photocatalytic properties of photocatalysts.

TiO2 is known to be the most widely used semiconductor in photocatalysis and solar energy conversion, which has been extensively studied in light of its abundance, nontoxicity, and stability. However, in practical applications, pure TiO2 is not a good candidate. Other than its disadvantages in high electron-hole recombination rate [15,16], low solar light utilization efficiency is another main restrictive factor [17]. In order to improve the photocatalytic efficiency of TiO2, researchers have found that photosensitization, co-catalyst, element doping, precious metal loading and construction of heterostructures are valid. However, the introduction of oxygen vacancy (V O) has enabled the visible light photoactivity of TiO2 had already been confirmed [18–21]. Investigations on photoinduced charge carrier dynamics over oxygen vacancy mediated TiO2 has been focused on inadequate issues, which are highly essential for exploring and optimizing efficient TiO2-V O photocatalysts.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a class of zero-dimensional (0D) graphene material, have attracted significant scientific interest in optoelectronic applications owing to its superior visible light absorbers with ultrafine size, high chemical stability, and tunable optical band gap [22–26]. Moreover, its extraordinary electronic properties [27–29] are conducive to photo-generated electronic transmission, which can be attributed to the crystal of sp2 carbon atoms arranged in a honeycomb lattice. Compared with graphene, the great advantage of GQDs lies in that its 0D feature can reduce light shielding and has the capacity to improve dispersity over other materials as cocatalyst. However, it has been proposed that GQDs can extends the light absorption of the TiO2 nanoparticles from UV to a wide visible region [30,31]. Till now, consensus regarding whether oxygen vacancy and lower potential energy are virtues or drawbacks for photocatalytic reactions has yet to be reached. Additionally, little attention has been devoted in investigating the transmission direction of photon-generated carriers between oxygen vacancy level and conduction bands.

In this study, 0D/2D nano-heterojunction of GQDs/TiO2 composites are obtained via simple ultrasonic self-assembly of TiO2 nanosheets and GQDs to boost photocatalytic redox reactions. UV–vis, photoluminescence (PL) spectra, photoelectric properties, and time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectroscopies are utilized in order to monitor the photoinduced charge carriers’ separation and recombination dynamics. Particularly, the transfer path of photogenerated charge carriers at interfaces between GQDs and TiO2 nanosheets accompanied with oxygen vacancy are also investigated. GQDs/TiO2 hybrid achieves more effective separation of carriers under visible light illumination, in which the defect level introduced by oxygen vacancy enables the facile transport of electrons through the hydrogen bond. Furthermore, the improved performance of photocatalytic synchronous removal of Cr (VI) and RhB can further verify electron activity and holds after separation. Accordingly, this study may provide insight into novel ideas for photocatalytic nano-heterojunction and defect level engineering.

Measured pyrene had to initially be nitrated into 1, 3, 6-trinitropyrene in hot nitric acid (HNO3). Next, alkaline hydrothermal treatment was performed in order to obtain the GQDs. TiO2 nanosheets with a size of 10–100 nm were then synthesized according to our previous method [16]. The GQDs/TiO2 composites were synthesized via ultrasonic and magnetic stirring to implement the self-assembly process. The mass ratio of GQDs and TiO2 was 1/100, for which the possible formation mechanism was illustrated in Scheme 1. Further procedural details are provided in Supporting Information. A commercial form of titanium dioxide (P25) served as the control experiments in following test.

The morphology and microstructure of photocatalysts were observed via transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Talos F200X GATAN 695. B) and Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM, BRUKER Multimode 8). The crystalline phases and chemical composition were characterized by a BRUKER D8 Advance XRD powder diffractometer, DXR 2 Raman and Perkin-Elmer Frontier FT-IR spectrometer with KBr pellets in the region 3000–400 cm−1. Electron Paramagnetic Resonance (EPR, BRUKER E500-9.5/12) spectrometer was used to verify oxygen vacancy. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a ULVAC -PHI 5000VPIII spectrometer. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra were obtained on a Shimadzu UV-3600 UV/vis/NIR spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) and time-resolved transient PL (TR-PL) spectra were collected on a Hitachi F-7000 and EDINBURGH FLSP920 spectrophotometer. The surface photovoltage (SPV) measurement used in the experiment was designed and assembled by our group. Photoelectrochemical experiments were performed in a three-electrode system (CHI-660E, Chenhua Instruments Co., Shanghai, China). A Pt wire and saturated calomel electrode (SCE) were used as counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. The catalyst powder was deposited on the fluoride tin oxide (FTO) substrate to serve as the working electrode. A 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution was then employed as the electrolyte. A 300 W Xenon lamp (MICROSOLAR 300UV, Beijing Perfect light) equipped with a 420 nm cutoff filter (k > 420 nm) was applied as a visible light source.

The photocatalytic activity of the synthesized multiple-effect photocatalysts was analyzed through the simultaneous removal of Cr (VI) (prepared by using K2Cr2O7 as the source of Cr (VI).) and RhB. Xe lamp (300 W) with UV cut off filter (λ > 420 nm) as irradiation source. In addition, about 20 mg of photocatalysts was dispersed in the 50 mL of solution simultaneous containing Cr (VI) ions (40 ppm) and RhB (10 ppm), respectively. Prior to irradiation, the suspension was stirred in the dark for 60 min to reach the adsorption-desorption equilibrium, after which the process continued for 60 min under visible light irradiation. Moreover, 3 mL of the solution was collected every 10 min, and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm to separate the photocatalyst. The clear solutions were then used to measure the of Cr (VI) and RhB content via UV–vis spectrometer, in which the wavelength of light absorbance λmax of Cr (VI), RhB, MB and Phenol in solutions were 356 nm, 554 nm, 664 nm and 370 nm.

In terms of structural characterization, GQDs/TiO2 had a similar XRD pattern (as shown in Fig. 1a) with pristine TiO2 assigned to anatase TiO2 [JCPDS: 21-1272] [32], here, the reflections of GQDs were unable to be distinguished due to their low degree of crystallinity and content. In addition, the crystallinity of the composite decreased due to the introduction of GQDs, and Raman spectroscopy provided information pertaining to the crystallinity/disorder, defects, degree of hybridization and the extent of chemical modification in the hybrid structures [33]. As shown in Fig. 1b, all of the TiO2 and GQD/TiO2 hybrid samples displayed the characteristic Raman bands of anatase TiO2 corresponding to three Eg and one A1g modes, which were consistent with literature [34]. Specifically, two additional peaks were observed at 1580 and 1363 cm−1, which correspond to the characteristic Raman D and G band of GQDs and arise from the in-plane vibration of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms as well as the edge states in graphene, respectively [35,36]. However, the D and G band in the GQDs/ TiO2 hybrid exhibited red shift compared to the GQDs, indicating the forces between the atoms existed.

In order to further gain insight into the GQDs/ TiO2 hybrid, EPR, FTIR and XPS spectra were recorded. As shown in the inset of Fig. 2a, pure TiO2 exhibited a very weak signal at g = 2.004, which can be ascribed to the signal of oxygen vacancies (V O) [37]. Surprisingly, GQDs/TiO2 demonstrated a remarkably strong EPR signal at g = 2.004, suggesting the introduction of V O during hybridization. Fig. 2b shows the FTIR spectra for GQDs, TiO2 and GQDs/TiO2 samples. The vibrational band lying in the range of 500–800 cm−1 can be attributed to the O-Ti-O bonds in the TiO2 component. As the enlarged view (Fig. 2c) illustrates, the Ti-O-Ti vibration observed at ∼1400 cm−1 in TiO2 [38] became prominent in GQDs/TiO2, which was caused by the lattice distortion due to the formation of a possible additive Ti-O bond or V O rearrangement during hybrid formation. More importantly, the broad peaks around 3430 cm−1 and 1633 cm−1 assigned to -OH vibration [39,40] became broader and weaker due to the effect of the hydrogen bond between TiO2 and GQDs. The proposed plausible mechanism is shown in Fig. 2d based on the obtained results. As expected, under the above connection, the ZETA potential (Fig. 2e and Fig. S1) of GQDs/ TiO2 hybrid decreased from −8.5 (TiO2) and −12.2 (GQDs) to −17.6 mV upon the formation of Vo, which was in line with EPR detection. The hydrogen bond between TiO2 and GQDs was further investigated using XPS analysis. In light of the above analysis, XPS spectra of Ti 2p (Fig. 2f) over GQDs/TiO2 and TiO2 fitted with two Gaussian peaks were at 458.5/458.4 and 464.2/464.1 eV, attributing to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 for Ti4+. Fig. 2g presents the deconvoluted high resolution O 1 s spectra of GQDs/TiO2 and TiO2, in which two identical energy peaks at 529.8 and 531.3 eV were assigned to Ti-O-Ti and O-H bonding in TiO2. Two additional peaks at 531.9 and 533.1 eV for GQDs/TiO2 can be attributed to O-C and V O, respectively [41]. The C 1s spectrum in Fig. 2h shows that peaks at 284.7 eV, 286.8 and 289.2 eV are referred to C-C or CC (graphite) and C-O. Notably, for GQDs/TiO2, the binding energies of O 1s and C1s shifted to lower binding energies compared with that of single TiO2, signifying a rise in electron concentration [42], which was consistent with the above analysis.

The size and morphological features of the TiO2 nanosheets, GQDs and their hybrids were then analyzed via transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) imaging. TEM images of TiO2 nanosheets, GQDs and GQDs/TiO2 with different magnifications are shown in Fig. 3. The HRTEM image in Fig. 3(b, e, g) illustrates lattice spacings of 0.35 nm and 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (101) facets of TiO2 and the (002) facets of GQDs, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3c, the GQDs were of nanodots with an average size of around 2.7 nm (inset in Fig. 3c). Importantly, the formation of GQDs/TiO2 hybrid was noticeable from the TEM/HRTEM images in Fig. 3(f-g), from which the dark spots corresponding to the GQDs were clearly observed on the exterior surface of TiO2 nanosheets. In particular, AFM imaging demonstrated that hybrid powders presented a rough thickness from 3.9 to 8.3 nm (Fig. 3h), which predicts that TiO2 nanosheets were superimposed by GQDs decorated facies with the neoformation of hydrogen bonding.

The optical absorption and band structure in all samples were analyzed according to the results of the UV–vis spectra, Kubelka-Munk plots and XPS valence band spectrum. As shown in Fig. 4a, due to the combined effect and oxygen vacancy defects, the band edge of GQDs/TiO2 red shifted with respect to that of TiO2. This extended absorption was attributed to the introduction of GQDs, and the enhanced absorption in the visible region of the hybrid may be advantageous for visible light photocatalysis. Furthermore, the intimate contact between GQDs and TiO2 through hydrogen bonds may facilitate the efficient interfacial charge transfer. As shown in Fig. 4c, the PL intensity of GQDs/TiO2 was found to be remarkably lower than that of the TiO2 nanosheets and P25, indicating the relatively reduced electron-hole recombination efficiency. Combining the band gaps (E g) for TiO2 and GQDs, which were calculated [43] to be 3.28 and 0.83 eV (Fig. 4b), respectively, the valance band (VB) positions were correspondingly found to be 2.46 eV and 1.62 eV (Fig. 4d-e). The schematic diagram of the band structure for TiO2 and GQDs is given in Fig. 4f.

Based on the above feature analysis, the photocatalytic properties of the materials synthesized (TiO2 nanosheets, GQDs/TiO2 composites) and purchased (P25) were evaluated under visible light. Their photocatalytic performances are outlined in Fig. 5, in which the P25 exhibited no response under visible light illumination. Closely related with the relatively high crystallinity and existence of oxygen vacancy, pure TiO2 nanosheets were shown to perform better than P25 under the same conditions. Notably, a substantial enhancement was elicited by the GQDs/TiO2 hybrid, which can be attributed to light absorption extension and the rise in charge separation efficiency. Compared with pure TiO2, two times the content of Cr (VI) and three times that of RhB were removed by GQDs/TiO2 after 60 min under visible light irradiation. The measuring of total organic carbon (TOC) (Fig. S2) confirmed the mineralization of RhB to CO2 and H2O in the photocatalytic degradation process. As described above, GQDs/TiO2 composites possessed superior visible light photocatalytic redox ability in contrast with that of both P25 and pure TiO2 nanosheets. Moreover, structure stability and performance stability of the GQDs/TiO2 composite that underwent photocatalytic reactions for a long duration of time were investigated by TEM imaging (Fig. S3) and cycling photocatalytic experiments (Fig. S5). In addition, the same trend was shown to occur in the photocatalytic removal of Cr (VI) and MB, Cr (VI) and Phenol under the above condition. (Fig. S5 and Table S1). These findings were in accordance with our expectations, nevertheless, further analyses are needed to elucidate the mechanism of charge transfer mode and subsequent photoreaction process on the GQDs/TiO2 heterojunction under visible light irradiation.

In order to gain further insight into the underlying charge recombination-transfer process and 0D/2D heterojunction structure-induced effects on the behaviors of photogenerated carriers, the time resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra of TiO2 and GQDs/TiO2 were recorded. As shown in Fig. 6a, the fluorescence intensities of both samples decayed exponentially. In contrast to the pure TiO2, GQDs/TiO2 exhibited slower decay kinetics. Notably, the difference in charge dynamics between the two decays were mainly in the exciton and free carrier recombination. Fitting the decay spectra showed two radiative lifetimes with corresponding percentages, as shown in Table 1. Here, the short lifetime (τ1) corresponded to the radiative recombination of the carriers, while the long lifetime (τ2) corresponded to non-radiative recombination [44–46]. In detail, τ2 was increased from 3.921 ns in pure TiO2 to 4.364 in GQDs/TiO2. Though τ1 of 0.951 ns decreased to 0.928 ns in GQDs/TiO2, the average lifetime (τAve) continued to grow (calculated with the equation given in the Supporting Information) to 2.44 ns for TiO2 and 2.65 ns for GQDs/TiO2, respectively, which can be regarded as a rational measure in evaluating the efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole separation [47,48]. Thus, the results signified that the recombination of TiO2 was inhibited via loading of GQDs, in which the selective electron migration property facilitated the separation and migration of photogenerated charges, resulting in a prolonged lifetime for the charge carriers.

In regard to the dynamic behaviors of the photogenerated charge carriers, a surface photovoltage (SPV) measurement was conducted so as to investigate the charge transfer process at the interface of the GQDs/TiO2 photocatalysts, in which the SPV response intensity was found to be related to the separation capability of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. In general, a stronger SPV response signal implies a higher separation efficiency under the illuminated condition. As shown in Fig. 6b, GQDs/TiO2 composites exhibited a higher SPV intensity than that of pure TiO2, indicating that this nano-heterojunction plays an important role in the transfer of photogenerated carriers. The conspicuously expedited charge separation in the GQDs/TiO2 hybrid can be assigned to the better transmission path of carriers caused by different photon excitation energy as described below.

To investigate the mechanism of the GQDs/TiO2 hybrid in photocatalytic redox reactions, transient photocurrent responses were used to analyze the efficiency of charge generation and separation under visible light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 7a, the photocurrent density of GQDs/TiO2 was noted to be about 2 times higher than that of TiO2 under visible light (400–800 nm) irradiation, which may be attributed to the lower electron-hole pairs recombination and more efficient charge carrier migration. GQDs had a better transient photocurrent response under visible light irradiation than UV irradiation (Fig. S6a), which may explain why the photocurrent density of TiO2 and GQDs/TiO2 were almost the same under UV (200–400 nm) light irradiation (Fig. S6b). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurement is a common method used to analyze the separation ability of photogenerated carriers. As shown in Fig. 7b, Nyquist plots of GQDs/TiO2 displayed a remarkable decrease in the radius of the arc compared with that of pure TiO2, demonstrating the lower transfer resistance and faster charge transfer kinetics for charge carriers of GQDs/TiO2. It can also be attributed to the construction of an efficient carrier transfer mechanism in the interface of GQDs/TiO2 heterojunction.

Generally, the Fermi energy (E F) of n-type semiconductors are considered to be located just below the conduction band [49]. The MS curves of all samples demonstrated positive slopes (Fig. S7), indicating TiO2 and GQDs were both typical n-type semiconductors. According to the obtained band structure, GQDs had a lower Fermi level than that of TiO2 prior to contact. Therefore, electrons may not transfer between them because of the band bending structures. As depicted in Fig. 8d, upon UV light excitation, the VB electrons jumped to CB of GQDs and TiO2, respectively. Differently, visible light irradiation only has the ability to excite electrons to the oxygen vacancy level of TiO2. Attributing to the efficient separation of electrons and holes, the photocatalytic redox activity doubled after the composite, which were tested before. In order to further explore the charge transfer and separation at GQDs/TiO2, the heterojunction interface between TiO2 and GQDs was simulated (Fig. S8). Fig. 8a presents the side views of the GQDs/TiO2 interface model used in the corresponding calculations. Moreover, the charge density difference of the GQDs/TiO2 heterostructure was calculated as shown in Fig. 8(b-c). The cyan and yellow areas represent electron depletion and accumulation, respectively. A significant variation of charge density was observed at the interface between GQDs and TiO2. Evidently, a strong charge accumulation was noted just above the O atoms at the top layer of TiO2 as well as on the higher side of GQDs; however, the regions of charge depletion appeared both on the lower side of the GQDs and on the Ti atoms at the top layer. Furthermore, the signals of DMPO-‧OH and DMPO-‧O2 − were not found (Fig. S9), demonstrating that e − and h+ were the predominant active species in this photocatalytic reaction system [50–52]. Based on the above analyses, a possible mechanism of GQDs/TiO2 hybrid in photocatalytic redox was proposed (Fig. 8d). First, GQDs served an important role in visible light adsorption and provided photocatalytic oxidizing active sites. Specifically, electrons on the CB of GQDs transmitted to TiO2 originate from visible light excitation through a medium of oxygen vacancy. The efficient separation of carriers was promotive in the subsequent photocatalytic reactions. On the other hand, GQDs was available for degradation for RhB via holes on VB. Meanwhile, its uniform distribution on TiO2 nanosheets favored the promotion of contact between the photocatalyst and reactant. Overall, GQDs/TiO2 performed better in photocatalytic synchronous removal of Cr (VI) and RhB under visible light irradiation, attributing to the intensely boosted transmission and separation of photoexcited electron-hole pairs at the 0D/2D heterojunction interface.

This study the obtained a GQDs/TiO2 composite and demonstrated an enhanced activity for the photocatalytic synchronous removal of Cr (VI) and RhB under visible light irradiation. The corresponding findings suggest that, due to the broader solar spectrum range and higher charge mobility, the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 is significantly enhanced by the presence of a GQDs cocatalyst. Further studies confirmed a point of view in the photogenerated carriers transport pathway, in which the charge transfer between GQDs and TiO2 was expedited via hydrogen bonding accompanied with oxygen vacancy under visible light irradiation. Moreover, their intimate contact through the 0D/2D heterojunction interface intensely boosted the transmission and separation of photoexcited electron-hole pairs. Accordingly, the proposed method may serve as a strategy in improving the photocatalytic efficiency of TiO2, which may be a candidate in creating additional efficient utilization of solar energy.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21676213, 21807087 and 22077099), the Natural Science Basic Research Plan in Shaanxi Province of China (No. 2021JQ-440 and 2020JM-422), Key Research and Development Program of Shaanxi (No. 2019KWZ-07), and the Key Science and Technology Innovation Team of Shaanxi Province (No. 2017KCT-37).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2021.161872.
